data.title: Book Review: A Dollar For Fifty Cents
data.description:
By Martin Fridson, CFA

Closed-end funds (CEFs) are “chronically mispriced by the market,” writes Michael Joseph, CFA, but for investors hoping to capitalize on that inefficiency, “simply buying a closed-end fund trading at a discount isn’t enough.” Just picking the funds with the deepest discounts to net asset value (NAV) or the highest yields, adds Joseph, is a “recipe for disaster.”

He further cautions that investing in a CEF in hopes that an activist investor will swoop in and close the gap between NAV and market price is “risky” and "speculative." Furthermore, says the Deputy Chief Investment Officer at Stansberry Asset Management, purchasing a CEF when it is initially offered is “irrational.” He also points out that when the Fed aggressively raised interest rates in 2022, several leveraged municipal bond CEFs' valuations were slashed nearly in half.

By thus dispelling expectations of easy money, the author of this 89-page book corrects any misapprehensions that might be induced by his title, A Dollar for Fifty Cents. That phrase also appears in a subheading of a section recounting how Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger’s purchase of 20 percent of the shares of Source Capital after the 1969-1970 market downturn drove the CEF nearly 50 percent below the value of its underlying assets.

Buffett and Munger ultimately doubled their money, but as Joseph remarks in an understatement about discounts to NAV, they "aren’t always as steep as 50%." In a fairer representation of the actual opportunity set, he cites research showing that the best CEF strategy is to buy at a 20 percent discount, with the objective of selling when the discount narrows to 15 percent.

A Dollar for Fifty Cents is written to be accessible to nonprofessional investors but provides information and insights that can benefit professionals who are not already intimately familiar with CEFs. Joseph summarizes the extensive literature on what academics view as the puzzle of why any CEF would ever trade at less than the value of its holdings. He discusses the comparatively recent emergence of CEFs with specified termination dates. That structure is designed to ensure that holders can cash in at the NAV at a time known in advance, but Joseph notes that the termination dates “can often be extended for a variety of reasons.” He also informs investors about free screening sites that can aid CEF selection. Helpful, too, are his warnings about funds with names that do not accurately describe their actual holdings, as well as the misleading distribution rates shown on some CEF factsheets.

As for the book’s subtitle, Proven Strategies to Outperform the Market with Closed-End Funds, Joseph references several studies that found superior returns for CEFs. Readers hoping to see a contemporary, attested, index-beating management record built exclusively on CEFs, however, will be disappointed. They must settle for the statement of foreword writer Rich Bello of Blue Ridge Capital that his firm “achieved great returns” and “invested in more than a few CEFs.”

Many money managers would agree, though, that closed-end funds can play a constructive role in investment portfolios. One important application is providing diversification within an income-focused portfolio that also contains assets such as bonds, preferred stocks, and REITs. CEFs that increase their distributions over time help income-focused investors to keep pace with inflation despite substantial allocations to fixed-income securities. Investors pursuing such a strategy will benefit greatly from Michael Joseph’s balanced account of CEFs’ virtues and pitfalls.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Maze Therapeutics: An Innovative, Nature-Driven Platform
data.description:
Intro

Maze Therapeutics (MAZE) is helping fill a fundamental inefficiency in current drug development. Genome-Wide Association Studies can identify thousands of loci associated with common diseases; however, the industry has often struggled to convert these signals into medicines because of the complexity and obscurity in some of these biological mechanisms. Maze's Compass platform addresses this bottleneck through a process that integrates human genetics with functional genomics to map the molecular differences in genetic variants. This allows Maze to identify rare mutations that naturally lower disease risks and allows them to engineer small molecules that mimic these protective effects. Their lead asset MZE829 represents a potential best-in-class therapy for APOL1-mediated kidney disease (AMKD). While Vertex Pharmaceuticals has a first-mover advantage, MZE829's dual inhibition strategy may offer superior efficacy compared to the rapid turnover of the APOL1 protein. Phase 2 HORIZON data is expected in Q1 FY2026.

The company's platform has also found many other important indications, such as MZE782, which is an oral inhibitor of SLC6A19 for phenylketonuria and Chronic Kidney Disease, which just posted good Phase 1 biomarker data. More importantly, the "Skinny Gene" program for obesity represents a high-value asset as it blocks the nutrient absorption transporter, which results in a change in how the body processes food instead of how much a person eats. Additionally, Maze's strategic decision to license MZE001 to Shionogi for $150M upfront demonstrates management's ability to monetize its assets. This, combined with recent equity raises, provides a cash runway into FY2028 and insulates the company from near-term dilution. With a fortress balance sheet, a validated platform, and a major catalyst just months away, MAZE represents a highly asymmetric opportunity where the market has yet to price in the true potential of its "nature-driven" platform.

The Compass Platform

To understand the real value of MAZE, you have to look beyond the individual assets and realize the platform that they are developing. Targets validated by human genetics have always demonstrated higher probabilities of success in clinical trials compared to traditional methods. Their Compass platform is designed to use this advantage. They start by identifying individuals who carry genetic mutations that protect them from diseases. They create a naturally made blueprint for drug design, and the goal then becomes mimicking that protective variant. For their APOL1 program, they identified a variant of a protein that naturally blocks the toxicity of high-risk APOL1 alleles. MAZE utilizes advanced functional genomics to identify modifier genes within these individuals, which can serve as novel drug targets. This approach significantly de-risks the portfolio because, by the time Maze enters the clinic, the target has already been validated in humans, and safety can be confirmed by the existence of healthy carriers.

MZE829

The target, APOL1, is a protein that evolved as a defense mechanism in people living in West Africa, protecting them against the parasite that causes African Sleeping Sickness. The human genome naturally selected for two specific variants of the APOL1 gene known as G1 and G2, which spread across the region. This variant gave individuals protection against Sleeping Sickness but came with a crippling cost, kidney toxicity. Patients with two copies of these risk variants (G1/G1, G2/G2, or G1/G2) are at exponentially higher risk for chronic kidney disease. The protein inserts itself into the membranes of the kidney's filtration cells and forms pores that allow for potassium to leak out, eventually killing the cell. These cells don't regenerate, and once they are lost, the kidney's filtration barrier is compromised, potentially leading to proteinuria and progressive kidney failure. The current frontrunner for treatment is Vertex Pharmaceuticals, with their drug inaxaplin. Their drug works like a plug; it stops the pore from leaking. Maze takes a more sophisticated approach.

Through their Compass platform, Maze identified a rare protective variant, N264K, which naturally prevents APOL1 toxicity. MZE829 was chemically engineered to copy the structural effects of this variant. The differentiation of this product is the dual mechanism of action. It plugs the pore just like Vertex's drug, but it also disrupts the assembly of the pores in the first place. The APOL1 is a fast-moving protein with a half-life of less than an hour. This means that if drug levels dip between doses, new pores can start to form instantly, and damage can resume. By stopping the assembly of new pores, Maze's drug prevents the toxic structures from forming in the first place. It's the difference between bucketing water out of a boat versus fixing the hole in the hull. It is expected that this mechanism can offer superior efficiency, and this will be ultimately tested in the Phase 2 HORIZON trial.

The primary endpoint in the study is safety and tolerability. A key differentiation in the setup of this study is the inclusion of patients with Type 2 diabetes and broader CKD phenotypes. Maze is aiming to validate MZE829 for a much larger market than its initial outset. If MZE829 shows efficacy in reducing proteinuria in a diabetic APOL1 patient, the TAM expands from thousands to hundreds of thousands.

In October FY2024, Maze reported data from its Phase 1 study. The drug was well-tolerated at single doses up to 480mg and multiple doses up to 350mg. Importantly, the drug demonstrated a half-life of 15 hours, supporting once-daily dosing. While Vertex will likely launch first, the nephrology market is highly sensitive to efficacy. Proteinuria reduction is the strongest predictor of delays in End-Stage Renal Disease. Vertex set a high bar with a ~47.6% reduction in their Phase 2 trials. However, if MZE829 can demonstrate a numerically superior reduction with a clean safety profile, it could become the first-line option. The dual mechanism is the scientific weighting that makes this scenario plausible. Furthermore, Maze's alternative may be deemed as safer as it targets the pores & assembly, which may yield different results.

The prevalence of the high-risk APOL1 genotype is ~13% among African Americans. This gives us an estimated 1 million individuals who potentially carry this variant. The lifetime risk of developing kidney disease for these carriers is estimated to be 20%, giving us an estimated active patient population of 200,000. We model a net price of $85,000 annually, given that similar genetic kidney therapies often command prices in the $100,000s. Assuming a launch in 2029 and a peak market penetration of 15%, MZE829 could generate risk-adjusted sales of ~$2.5B in the U.S. alone. This figure excludes the potential upside from European and South American markets that have significant West African populations.

MZE782

Moving further down in their pipeline, we find MZE782. This is an oral treatment for Phenylketonuria (PKU), a rare disease where patients cannot break down an amino acid called phenylalanine (Phe), leading to brain toxicity. Current treatments are very strict as they require unpalatable diets or injections that carry a risky black box warning. MZE782 offers a simple oral solution that works for all patients, regardless of their specific mutation. MZE782 inhibits a transporter in the kidney called SLC6A19. This transport's job is to recycle Phe back into the blood. By blocking this, the drug essentially opens a release valve and allows the patient to simply pee out the excess Phe. Recent Phase 1 data confirmed this mechanism worked and showed a massive 42-fold increase in urinary Phe excretion. With ~16,500 PKU patients in the U.S. and a significant portion dissatisfied with current options, MZE782 could capture a substantial market share.

We estimate that 70% of patients have phenylalanine levels exceeding recommended targets despite current treatment and a 25% market penetration of those interested in the drug. Assuming a net price of $150,000 per year, given its orphan drug designation, peak sales could reach $433 million. The second indication for MZE782 is far larger. In Chronic Kidney Disease, the proximal tubule cells of the kidney are overworked. They expend massive amounts of energy and absorb filtered nutrients. This metabolic stress leads to hypoxia (oxygen starvation), oxidative stress, and cellular senescence. SGLT2 inhibitors, which are the current standard of care reduces the workload by reabsorbing amino acids. The hypothesis is that MZE782 will provide a similar benefit by reducing the metabolic demands of these fragile cells.

In the Phase 1 trial, MZE782 caused a dose-dependent dip in filtration rate, which is a signal of reduced pressure. This "dip" de-risks the CKD program significantly and signals that MZE782 could be used as a hemodynamic modulator. If developed as an add-on to existing SGLT2 inhibitors for non-diabetic CKD or for patients who respond negatively to SGLT2s, the market opportunity is in the billions. However, we aggressively risk-adjust this indication and treat it more as a free call option in their portfolio due to the large, expensive trials that would be required for approval. We utilized Guggenheim's estimates for the addressable market of this indication of 1.3 million patients. We estimate a price point of $1,080 per year, aligning with the pricing of other SGLT2 inhibitors. We expect a 20% penetration of the 1.3 million sub-segment, which could bring in around $280M at peak sales. In total, we estimate the peak sales of MZE782, including both indications, to be $713M.

GPR75

Perhaps the most exciting asset in their entire portfolio is the GPR75 program. In a massive study of 640,000 people, scientists found that individuals with "broken" GPR75 genes were naturally thin. They weighed 12 pounds or less on average and had a 54% lower risk of obesity. Mouse models have confirmed this causality, and the GPR75 mutation led to 44% less weight on a high-fat diet compared to wild-type mice. GPR75 is heavily expressed in the hypothalamus, which is primarily used for controlling appetite. While giants like Regeneron and AstraZeneca are chasing this target with complex therapies, Maze is using its biological engine to develop a daily pill based on nature's solution. Currently, the GPR75 program is in the preclinical stage, but the strategic value is immense. This is effectively a giant call option in their portfolio as it's in the very early stages of development, but could potentially unlock a slice of the trillion-dollar obesity market with a mechanism that works entirely different from current GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic.

MZE001 and the Shionogi Deal

The Shionogi deal and the MZE001 asset serve as the financial anchor behind the company. MZE001 is an oral inhibitor of Glycogen Synthase. In Pompe disease, patients lack the enzyme to break down glycogen, leading to toxic buildup in muscles. MZE001 works by slowing down the production of glycogen, reducing the burden on the muscles. Maze originally agreed to license this asset to Sanofi for $150M upfront, but the FTC sued to block the deal, arguing it would give Sanofi a monopoly on Pompe treatments. This confirmed the asset's high value. In May of FY2024, Maze successfully re-licensed MZE001 to Shionogi in a deal that allows for up to $605M in milestone payments and tiered royalties. We assume that MAZE will receive a standard royalty payment of ~12%. With the Pompe disease market estimated to grow to $2.32B by 2029, if MZE001 captures 20% of the market share, then this is a potential $464M revenue opportunity. The deal brought in massive non-dilutive capital and validated the asset's potential. Maze now retains a passive interest in MZE001 while Shionogi funds all future development.

Financial Snapshot

Maze Therapeutics is prepared to take advantage of its portfolio, as it has spent quality time building a fortress balance sheet that distinguishes it from its peers. Given their two active clinical programs, we can project an annual burn rate of $110-$130M for FY2026 and FY2027. As of Q3 FY2025, they have ~$380M in cash with ~$25M in debt, which provides them with a cash runway well into FY2028. This financial stability is also backed by their licensing deal with Shionogi for the Pompe disease asset, MZE001. After receiving a $150M upfront payment, Maze now retains a passive financial interest in the program. This means that Shionogi foots the bill for future development while Maze receives their remaining milestone payments and tiered royalties. This deal, once activated, acts as a high-margin revenue stream that costs Maze nothing. The combination of a heavy cash reserve and the potential for royalty payments insulates shareholders from near-term financing risks as they await major clinical data readouts.

Valuation

We expect an FY2028 launch for MZE829 and a FY2030 launch for MZE782. For MZE829, we estimate peak sales of $2.5B and assign a PoS of ~40% given their strong human genetic validation, giving us a rNPV of $1.6B. For MZE782, we estimate peak sales of $713M and assign a PoS of ~30% given the strong data from Phase 1 trials, but length trials are ahead of us, giving us a rNPV of $213M. The peak sales of MZE001 are estimated to be $464M, and we expect a blended average royalty rate of ~12%, giving us an estimated annual revenue contribution of ~$56M per year.

If we value these royalties on a 10-year horizon and discount them by 12%, then we arrive at ~$490M of intrinsic value for just royalty payments. If we also consider the milestone payments that they will receive and estimate an overall PoS of ~40%, the Shionogi deal represents ~$438M in intrinsic value. We also provide a conservative estimate of $400M for their early-stage obesity assets and the inherent value of their Compass engine. The equity value, including the cash position, is then estimated to be $3.01B. With 55.4M shares outstanding, this comes out to ~$54.33 per share. We view this as a fairly conservative estimate given the unmodeled pipeline optionality that comes with their innovative platform.

Sum-Of-Parts Calculation:

Asset Indication Est. Peak Sales Probability of Success (PoS) rNPV ($M) Per Share Value
MZE829 APOL1 Kidney Disease $2.5B 40% $1,600 $28.88
MZE782 PKU & CKD $713M 30% $213 $3.84
Shionogi Deal / MZE001 Pompe Disease $464M + Milestone Payments 40% $438 $7.90
Compass / GPR75 Platform / Indication N/A N/A $400 $7.22
Net Cash (Cash - Debt) N/A N/A $355 $6.40
Total - - - $3,006 $54.33

Risks

While the thesis behind MAZE is quite compelling, there are many risks that are still on the table. The valuation is heavily tied to MZE829 as of now, and if the Q1 FY2026 data fails to show meaningful proteinuria reduction or reveals unexpected safety problems, the stock could drop significantly. The dual mechanism is scientifically sound but remains unproven in a clinical setting. Vertex is also a formidable competitor with deep pockets and a largely built-out commercial infrastructure. Even if MZE829 is "best-in-class", Maze may run into trouble when competing with Vertex's marketing resources.

Additionally, the GPR75 asset is of very high value but remains in very early stages of development. This asset may not generate any meaningful momentum in the stock price until there are some concrete catalysts on the table that are moving it forward. Lastly, the value of MZE001 is entirely out of Maze's hands. If Shionogi mismanages the clinical development or commercial launch, its high-margin royalty revenues will disappear.

Conclusion

Maze Therapeutics represents a high-conviction opportunity in the genetic medicine space. By identifying protective variants found in nature, the company has built a pipeline that is inherently de-risked compared to traditional biotech peers. The dual mechanism of MZE829 offers a plausible path towards displacing Vertex in the multi-billion-dollar kidney disease market. The hidden value of GPR75 will eventually be uncovered as it moves into later stages of development, and until then, the Q1 FY2026 HORIZON readout will serve as the primary catalyst for price appreciation for this name. Overall, MAZE appears to be a heavily mispriced asset within the biotechnology space due to its de-risked pipeline and innovative platform, which fundamentally shifts the probability of clinical success in its favor.

data.title: Compounding Opportunity
data.description:
Economic outlook takeaways

  • Growth remains surprisingly resilient

After global growth withstood tariff pressures in 2025, the near-term outlook appears stronger, aided by AI-related capital spending and efficiency gains. Lower Chinese export prices have helped ease the shift in trade flows away from the U.S.

  • Winners and losers drive “K-shaped” economic trends

In the U.S., capital-intensive companies deploying AI are better positioned, as are wealthier households that benefit from tech-driven stock market gains. Others are increasingly at risk of being left behind. Globally, trade frictions, AI adoption, and associated policy responses are driving divergence among countries.

  • Global monetary and fiscal policies are moving in different directions

The U.K. and several emerging market (EM) economies with high real rates and limited fiscal space are likely to ease monetary policy more than the European Central Bank or Bank of Canada, where policy is already neutral. Fiscal policy is set to become more influential in China amid trade pressures, and in the U.S. where tax cuts will likely boost households and businesses.

Investment outlook takeaways

  • Bonds present compelling and durable opportunities

Active fixed income strategies delivered their best results in years in 2025, and the outlook for 2026 is just as compelling. The post-pandemic bond market repricing set up an extended period of attractive starting yields. Divergent economic conditions offer abundant avenues for active managers to generate alpha, or outperformance versus the broader market. Investors have a rare opportunity to increase quality and liquidity without giving up equity-like return potential – at a time when equity valuations have reached extremes.

  • Use global diversification to help mitigate risks

Diverse economic and policy conditions across countries present investment opportunities in both developed markets (DM) and EM. Notably, several large EM economies offer significant real yield premiums over DM bonds – compensation for risks that are increasingly idiosyncratic and diversifiable rather than systemic. EM local currency bonds delivered strong returns in 2025 while providing crucial portfolio diversification at a time when DM correlations remain elevated.

  • Choose investments carefully in a late-cycle credit environment

We favor high quality areas such as securitized credit and asset-based finance, which benefit from robust lending standards and strength among higher-income consumers. We are prioritizing security selection and reducing more generic credit and private corporate credit exposure.

Economic outlook: Dimensions of divergence

The Trump administration’s sweeping tax, trade, and immigration policy overhauls – including quadrupling the effective U.S. tariff rate – were widely expected to stifle global growth, trade and investment. In response, various DM and EM governments announced preemptive yet targeted fiscal measures to buffer the economic transitions, while central banks focused on downside risks.

It turns out that economic growth has been surprisingly resilient as these policy trends intersected with a new general-purpose technology: AI. The result has been a lasting expansion with notable divergence under the surface. “K-shaped” economic trends are apparent across households, companies, and regions. Indeed, those who are better positioned to benefit from the AI race and associated wealth effects are fueling growth.

Several key macro trends have unfolded:

  • In the U.S., elevated competition has limited corporate pricing power and muted tariff-related price increases. Large companies are focusing on gaining market share by competing on price and absorbing tariff costs, fueling a productivity push to protect margins. Small and midsize labor-intensive companies exposed to trade sectors or immigration policy changes have been relative losers.
  • To defend margins, companies accelerated AI adoption to manage labor costs. In the U.S., AI-related software and R&D investment accelerated, while data center investment, including structures, servers, chips, and other components doubled.
  • Policy and technology changes also contributed to a U.S. household divide. The wealth effect of AI-led stock market gains has helped sustain consumption, but the benefits haven’t reached lower- and middle-income households (see Figure 1). As elevated uncertainty has stifled hiring and trade- and AI-exposed sectors shed jobs, real labor income growth has stalled. The AI related build-out also appears to be crowding out other investment, including residential housing, further reducing housing affordability.

Figure 1: Equity-based wealth gains have recently outpaced labor income growth

Source: Haver Analytics, PIMCO calculations as of 30 September 2025

  • AI trends have supported global industrial output and trade despite tariff drags, although gains remain uneven. Growth is concentrated in computers and components tied to AI infrastructure. Asian economies, including Taiwan, Japan, South Korea, and China, have shared the U.S.’s resilience as they dominate production of chips, servers, and related hardware. Production elsewhere has slowed as earlier inventory stocking ahead of tariffs unwinds.
  • China is being pushed to find other markets for its goods, while also accelerating its AI infrastructure build-out and further improving manufacturing productivity. U.S. tariffs have reduced trade between the two nations. Lower export prices have facilitated a smoother-than-expected trade rotation to EM. Still, sluggish consumption and falling investment have left China overly reliant on exports and inventory building to maintain growth.

Technology and fiscal policy bolster demand

We expect overall economic resilience to continue in 2026. There are also good reasons to expect some broadening in growth, but the trend toward winners and losers is likely to persist.

First, fiscal policy is set to diverge across countries. Fiscal policy easing in several regions should further offset trade drags. China, Japan, Germany, Canada, and the U.S. are all poised to loosen fiscal policy – China through central government support, and the U.S. via large, front-loaded business and household tax cuts. But many countries lack fiscal space, leaving policy tight in the U.K., France, and parts of EM.

Second, the AI investment cycle should keep supporting global growth as AI adoption spreads, but winners and losers will abound. In the U.S., broader corporate spending on AI implementation, software, and R&D could offset cooling data‑center capital spending from high 2025 levels. An additional tailwind could come from other countries stepping up infrastructure investment amid national security concerns. In the race for AI dominance, regional and industry laggards are at risk.

Third, trade uncertainty and tariff-related drags should also diminish in 2026, but not without further policy shifts as the legality of U.S. tariffs is tested. The U.S. Supreme Court could potentially strike down some or all tariffs implemented under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act. As the Trump administration shifts tariff policy to a more stable and legally secure framework, various regions and sectors will need to adjust, while reduced uncertainty reaccelerates investment and hiring, both in the U.S. and globally.

Monetary policy set to diverge

Most central banks embarked on rate-cutting cycles over the past few years, albeit at differing speeds based on inflation progress. With global inflation now broadly benign, we expect most central banks to reach neutral policy levels by the end of 2026. However, the outlook for additional cuts is now more nuanced.

Central banks with still-high real rates and tight fiscal policy are poised to cut more aggressively, particularly in countries more exposed to downside inflation risks from Chinese exports. This includes various EM central banks, as well as the Bank of England.

Elsewhere, where monetary policy is already near neutral and fiscal policy is poised to expand – notably Canada and, to a lesser extent, Europe – there is limited need for additional cuts. The Bank of Japan, meanwhile, with still-easy monetary policy and where fiscal policy is set to expand, is expected to hike rates further (see Figure 2), while in China both monetary and fiscal policy are expected to ease substantially, as policymakers manage debt deflation and overcapacity.

Figure 2: We see increased likelihood of policy divergence in 2026

Source: PIMCO as of December 2025

Finally, markets are pricing expectations for the U.S. Federal Reserve to lower its policy rate further, to 3%. We also expect Fed cuts in 2026, likely in the latter half of the year.

Uncertainty remains about the terminal rate amid a transition to a new Fed chair and as the White House pushes for lower rates. While there is a range of possible outcomes, the market has consistently priced a continuation with orthodoxy, reflecting the fairly conventional candidates and checks and balances inherent in the Fed policy-setting process.

The risks around U.S. inflation also look more two-sided. AI-fueled productivity and stagnant housing market trends could help keep overall prices in check. Tariffs, demand-augmenting fiscal policy, and technological infrastructure buildout could push prices higher.

Durability alongside vulnerability

While we expect economic resilience to continue, clashing forces and widespread haves-versus-have-nots dynamics create risks:

  • U.S. risk-asset valuations: Traditional valuation metrics suggest U.S. stocks are expensive, both relative to history and to other markets. How much AI adoption accelerates and how much value can be created by AI (and when) – along with which companies will capture that value – remain key questions. Meanwhile, credit spreads continue to look tight.
  • Sustainability of the K-shaped economy: Wealth-fueled consumption depends on additional equity and housing market appreciation, but high valuations and affordability pressures make this challenging. Areas of private credit look particularly vulnerable to policy- and AI-related transformations.
  • Government deficit and debt dynamics: We haven’t changed our secular outlook for challenging debt and deficit dynamics across many DM economies, including the U.S., U.K., France, and Japan (for more, see our June 2025 Secular Outlook, “The Fragmentation Era”). While debt appears sustainable now – the average interest rate paid on government debt is still below trend growth levels – AI and trade policies could drive investment trends that prompt higher interest rates, adding pressure to sovereign debt.
  • China challenges: A multiyear housing sector bust and already high global manufacturing share add to questions about how long China can sustain its production- and export-led growth model. Without materially more direct central government support for domestic demand, China will find it harder to reach growth targets, with disinflationary implications for the rest of the world.

Investment implications: Take advantage of the fixed income opportunity

After years of strong risk-asset returns, equity valuations remain elevated and credit spreads are tight. While our base case calls for growth to remain solid and potentially even reaccelerate in some regions, such optimism is already priced into most risk-asset markets. History suggests that these starting valuations will influence forward returns, which may be lower than investors have come to expect.

By contrast, bonds are cheap versus stocks at current valuations. After a sharp post-pandemic repricing, starting yields on high quality bonds remain attractive, highlighting the sustainable return potential in fixed income. Investors today have a rare opportunity to increase quality, liquidity, and portfolio diversification without giving up equity-like return potential.

Even after broadly strong bond market returns in 2025, the yield on the benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury note – about 4.19% as of 12 January 2026 – remains in the middle of the 3.5%–5% range that it has occupied for more than three years. Other DM sovereign 10-year yields tell a similar story (see Figure 3). This illustrates that robust bond returns don’t depend on a broad rally in rates.

Figure 3: Various 10-year sovereign bonds offer attractive yields

Source: PIMCO and Bloomberg as of 31 December 2025

Rather, attractive starting yields provide a baseline from which active managers can seek to construct portfolios potentially yielding about 5%–7% by capitalizing on alpha opportunities. (Learn more in our recent article, “Calculating the Active Advantage in Fixed Income.”) Active fixed income strategies delivered their best results in years in 2025 – and the outlook ahead is just as compelling amid one of the most exciting environments for alpha generation in recent memory.

Our 2026 playbook remains similar to 2025 in many respects. Amid a generally benign global growth outlook, and with attractive yields available in many countries, we favor a diversified portfolio of exposures across regions with different economic and policy paths, including DM and select EM local markets. Overall, our approach remains flexible. We expect to add and trim exposures based on valuations and market dislocations.

Rates, duration, and global opportunities

As yield curves have steepened, we believe investors who continue holding excess cash are missing a potential opportunity. By turning to fixed income, which has outperformed cash, investors can lock in more attractive yields over a longer period while also benefiting from potential price appreciation for a modest increase in risk.

We maintain a modest overweight to duration – a gauge of interest rate exposure – with a focus on global diversification. (Explore other opportunities to bolster portfolio diversification and resilience in our recent article, “Charting the Year Ahead: Investment Ideas for 2026.”) While we continue to favor 2- to 5-year bond maturities, our curve positioning has grown more balanced as longer-term yields have become more attractive.

U.S. duration still looks attractive and can help hedge portfolios against a potential slowdown in the U.S. labor market or AI-related equity volatility. European duration looks comparatively less attractive.

While Australian duration has underperformed, it remains a useful diversifier within a broader basket, especially now that markets are pricing in potential rate hikes in 2026 – a policy move we believe is unlikely.

Despite inflation above central bank targets and near-term risk of reacceleration, longer-term breakevens remain low. We continue to like Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS), commodities, and real asset exposures.

We see select opportunities in countries with higher real policy rates, tighter fiscal settings, and more balanced inflation risks. This includes the U.K. and select EM countries.

Emerging markets: Asymmetric opportunities in a fragmented world

The EM investment landscape has structurally transformed, with lower aggregate government debt-to-GDP than DM, improved monetary policy frameworks and current accounts, and deepening local capital markets. In a twist, several advanced economies now exhibit fiscal dynamics once considered “EM-style risks.”

For active managers, dispersion creates opportunity. Unlike the 2010s when EM moved as a bloc, today’s environment rewards granular country selection across rates, currencies, and credit – generating alpha through structural analysis rather than beta timing. Across EM, we find attractive starting yields and a variety of idiosyncratic, diversifiable risks.

EM central banks are expected to continue to cut rates amid low inflation and resilient foreign exchange. We prefer duration overweights in South Africa and Peru, where yield curves are steeper than domestic fundamentals warrant, and Brazil, where we see room for a large and extended rate-cutting cycle.

We continue to see the potential for U.S. dollar weakness, reflecting the ongoing Fed easing cycle, secular fiscal concerns, and starting valuations that favor an overweight to EM currencies versus DM counterparts. EM currencies provide a liquid way to access the asset class outside of our dedicated EM strategies, and we can potentially generate attractive income with a carefully managed and well-diversified basket of EM country exposures.

Credit: Constructive but selective

Our stance toward credit remains constructive but has grown more selective. At PIMCO, we have witnessed many credit cycles across the five-plus decades since the firm’s founding. We are seeing signs of later-cycle behavior as strong recent returns have fueled complacency.

We expect continued deterioration in credit fundamentals, especially in floating-rate sectors of corporate markets, given weaker underwriting standards in recent years. Industry and single name exposure will matter, as we see fundamental pressure in areas such as healthcare, retail, and technology.

We have seen an increase in amendment activity, such as payment in kind (PIK) provisions that allow borrowers to repay debt with more debt. Such trends can mask underlying stress by keeping headline default rates low (see Figure 4).

Figure 4: Low headline default rates can mask underlying credit stress

Source: Bank of America as of March 2025, based on latest publicly available data

We have also observed overreliance on rating agency ratings as a barometer for risk, and vehicles promising more liquidity than their underlying investment strategies may be able to deliver. These conditions are occurring in the wake of rapid growth in private credit markets in recent years.

During such periods, we look to reduce generic credit exposure – or beta – and focus on independent, bottom-up analysis and security selection.

We continue to favor U.S. agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS). Agencies remain a preferred partial substitute for corporate credit beta, supported by strong structural features, robust liquidity, and attractive spreads.

Rather than regarding credit markets as separate public and private segments, we continue to evaluate investments along continuums of economic sensitivity and liquidity risk, and we focus on ensuring adequate compensation for these risks. We consider the reasons companies turn to private versus public credit, such as greater flexibility or less restrictive regulation, and what that means for investors.

Investment grade issuers with stable cash flow and strong balance sheets remain the core of our credit positioning. We value the robust liquidity in public investment grade markets and believe investors should be selective when venturing into private investment grade, especially when incremental spread over more liquid opportunities is limited.

We continue to seek unique, well-structured credit opportunities that leverage PIMCO’s scale. We look to avoid lower-quality deals with less attractive spreads, weak collateral, and fewer lender protections. We expect secured lending in areas such as asset-based finance, real estate credit, and well-structured infrastructure debt to outperform. Lower-quality segments of corporate markets are more likely to disappoint given tight spreads, weak underwriting, and broader signs of overall complacency.

We continue to see value in areas offering robust collateral and clear structural protections. We see these opportunities in both liquid securitized markets and less liquid asset-based finance areas, especially those linked to higher-income consumers. Real estate debt, while out of favor, benefits from asset values that are well below peak levels.

Within high yield markets, we are cautious where covenant erosion or sponsor behavior creates greater downside risk. Direct lending, bank loans, and weaker high yield segments require particular caution, given questions about the quality of lender safeguards and potential liquidity challenges. Excess capital formation in these markets has resulted in programmatic lending activity that resembles more passive investment strategies.

Conclusion

In recent decades, abundant capital, low interest rates, and a stable global order reduced the need for diversification. In contrast, today’s environment is defined by dispersion, two-way risk, and economies across regions moving at different speeds. This creates a wide range of opportunities across global rates, EM, high quality credit, and securitized markets, reinforcing the value of an active approach.

data.title: Constellation Energy: A Growth-Oriented Nuclear Buy In A Power-Hungry Economy
data.description:
Thesis

I'm keeping my "BUY" recommendation on Constellation Energy (CEG) because I believe it is the only large cap option that offers exposure to carbon-free electric power in the United States.

I've covered the company before, and I'm still bullish because the investment case is not only intact but is reinforced by structural developments in energy consumption.

CEG has some qualities that give it a growth profile in what's typically seen as a defensive sector, such as nuclear scale, solid assets, and strong cash generation.

What makes CEG different is not just that it is nuclear-based but the fact that it operates as a merchant power producer.

Unlike regulated utilities with capped returns, the company plays directly in energy pricing.

Company Overview: What CEG Really Is

Constellation Energy is the biggest nuclear power operator in the U.S. It has a portfolio of units that operate continuously, day and night, regardless of the weather or fluctuations in other energy sources.

These units deliver power you can actually count on. This is especially important in a grid that is often left hanging by a thread from renewable energy sources.

As I see it, CEG's assets are not just important, but they are strategically irreplaceable.

What makes the story more interesting is the company's business model. CEG sells its energy in competitive electricity markets where revenues are directly affected by demand and prices.

This means greater volatility but also real potential for profit growth when consumption increases.

According to my perspective, this flexibility is part of the reason why CEG is valued as a growth platform and not just a utility.

In recent developments, as I will analyze below, CEG has deepened this story of stability and strategic value.

The company completed the acquisition of Calpine and created the largest power generation portfolio in the U.S. with the expansion of its energy infrastructure base (natural gas, hydro, and geothermal, beyond its nuclear fleet).

This move strengthens the scale and revenue stability, which I see as a major strategic progress in the effort to meet growing electricity demand.

The NRC (Nuclear Regulatory Commission) approved a $167 million digital modernization project at the Limerick Clean Energy Center, improving reliability, diagnostics, and grid security resiliency.

These technology upgrades surround the production with durability and long-term sustainability, which I consider important for a company that promises continuity and stability.

This, I am convinced, gives CEG not just revenue but a strategic role in the global AI energy ecosystem.

From my point of view, these developments show a company profile that uses the nuclear base as a growth platform for greater scale and diversification.

This flexibility in the business model is one of my main personal arguments in favor of CEG in this investment category.

Market Context: Why Nuclear Is Back

Now let's talk about the energy market.

The energy market in the United States and internationally is in a phase of structural change, where the demand for electricity is growing at a pace that was not expected a few years ago.

According to estimates of the U.S. Energy Information Administration, total electricity consumption in the United States will break new records and is expected to continue to grow until 2027.

A big part of this increase comes from data centers and other energy-intensive technological applications.

From a global perspective, the International Energy Agency reports that total electricity demand grew by 4% in 2024, much faster than the average in recent years, mainly due to increasing electrification, digitalization, and electrical applications.

One of the most powerful drivers of this growth is the rapid expansion of data centers (especially those supporting artificial intelligence applications).

Studies show that data center electricity consumption could double or even triple by 2028 putting significant strain on the existing power grid and adding needs that traditional infrastructure struggles to meet.

At the same time, renewable energy sources like solar and wind depend on weather conditions and day/night cycles.

This in turn increases the need for steady generation sources to ensure adequate power across the full spectrum of demand.

Nuclear energy fills this gap by offering carbon-free generation with a stable power supply regardless of weather or operating hours and therefore acts as a reliable foundation in an energy system facing increasing needs.

In this context, CEG sits right at the center of this shift as it has installed and operational assets without the need for multiyear investments or permitting of new projects.

Personally, I think this advantage remains undervalued by the market, given the outlook for larger and sustained electricity demand.

Financials & Earnings Power: Beyond Utility Thinking

The company announced strong EBITDA and Free Cash Flow while EBITDA grew by 15%-20%.

This development is linked to the rise in electricity demand and the company's position in the baseload market. If you ask me, this is the central pillar of the investment case.

The hedging strategy offers clear short term profit visibility, as a significant part of the production is already secured at known prices.

At the same time, a substantial percentage of production is exposed to upward movements in energy prices.

Management demonstrates discipline in capital allocation with share buyback programs that have reduced the number of shares by a mid-single digit percentage in recent years.

This strategy enhances value per share and supports long term EPS growth, and I see it as a sign of confidence in the sustainability and quality of cash flows.

In Q32025, the results helped me form a clearer and more measurable view of CEG's growth story in a measurable and not theoretical way.

The company announced EPS of $3.04 and GAAP EPS of $2.97, with adjusted EPS up $0.30 year-over-year, which I attribute primarily to better operational execution.

In my opinion, the strongest point of the quarter is the nuclear operation. It has a capacity factor of 96.8%, which is 4% above the average of the sector. At the same time, the company tightened its 2025 guidance to $9.05-$9.45 per share on a standalone basis. This gives confidence for the rest of the year.

From an investor’s perspective, what stands out is CEG’s ability to combine strong current operating performance with substantial financial flexibility, highlighted by approximately $14 billion in liquidity expected post-Calpine, which supports capital returns, balance sheet resilience, and continued execution of its long-term growth strategy.

Valuation: Paying For Quality And Growth

Constellation Energy's valuation already embeds a premium versus traditional utilities, a premium I consider justified given the asset quality, nuclear base, and growth profile.

At the current share price of $333, CEG trades at approximately 35x forward earnings, based on the midpoint of its 2025 standalone EPS guidance of $9.25.

I use P/E as the primary valuation framework because the company has transitioned into a phase of strong and predictable profitability supported by recurring cash flows.

In the bear case, I assume earnings growth moderates and valuation compresses toward 28x earnings, still a premium to regulated utilities but more consistent with a market pricing in higher volatility and slower contracting momentum.

Applying a 28x multiple to $9.50 of normalized earnings results in a target price of approximately $265, representing roughly 20% downside from current levels.

In the base case, I assume steady earnings growth in line with management guidance, continued capital discipline, and no major acceleration in hyperscaler contracting.

Under these assumptions, I view a 38x multiple as reasonable, reflecting CEG’s superior asset quality and merchant exposure.

Applying this multiple to earnings of roughly $9.60 supports a price target near $365, or about 10% upside.

In the bull case, accelerating demand from AI-driven data centers and long-term power purchase agreements materially improve earnings visibility and quality.

In this scenario, CEG increasingly resembles a long-duration growth compounder within the energy sector.

I apply a 49x multiple to $9.50–$9.70 of earnings power, resulting in a target price of approximately $465, implying upside of about 40%.

In general, CEG has limited downside and good upside, based on long-term drivers.

For that reason, I keep a "BUY" recommendation and see the premium as fair for a quality growth company.

The stock already shows this positive trend in the market. It has been going up for years and is up about 640% in the last four years.

The 18% drop from the highs gives a first entry point. The second one is near the 260 area.

Risks: What Could Go Wrong

The most critical risks for Constellation have to do with the regulatory environment and the nature of nuclear generation.

As we know, nuclear facilities face strict oversight, and any shift in regulation or licensing can increase costs and hurt profitability.

Additionally, political support for nuclear is more positive, but it can still change with social and political events.

Another key risk is the unexpected shutdown of the nuclear plants. This could reduce production, increase costs, and worsen the financial results. Volatility of the electricity prices creates uncertainty too, as it affects the revenues of a merchant company.

Conclusion

The most critical risks for Constellation have to do with the regulatory environment and the nature of nuclear generation.

As we know, nuclear facilities face strict oversight, and any shift in regulation or licensing can increase costs and hurt profitability.

Additionally, political support for nuclear is more positive, but it can still change with social and political events.

Another key risk is the unexpected shutdown of the nuclear plants.

This could reduce production, increase costs, and worsen the financial results. Volatility of the electricity prices creates uncertainty too, as it affects the revenues of a merchant company.

data.title: ATI: A Stock With Potential But Prudent Timing Is Advised
data.description:
Investment Thesis

ATI Inc. (ATI) is an interesting opportunity in the aerospace and defense segment, with significant sector tailwinds as the Trump administration is massively ramping up spending in defense, and in particular in US manufacturers. ATI specialises in titanium production, as well as the production of nickel alloys, titanium alloys, and other types of alloys essential to the US defense. These alloys are critical for US defense jet engines, being of national interest. The largest global producer and supplier of the US of titanium is China, and the US, the EU and Japan classify titanium as a “critical mineral.”

Securing the supply chain of titanium all the way until the jet engine production is of national interest for the US. The Australian company IperionX Limited (IPX) rallied in 2025 in expanding its titanium mining efforts within US borders. ATI is a little bit further down the line of the supply chain, where it uses the raw titanium to transform it into alloys and produce jet engines in solid partnerships with the Western duopoly for planes, The Boeing Company (BA) and Airbus SE (EADSF) (EADSY). The company clearly has strong tailwinds and a solid customer base, but while fundamentals are solid, the valuation is somewhat stretched, and I will rate ATI a “Buy,” but a buy on the next dip.

Business Overview

ATI’s core business is not per se tied to US national interest. It’s a company with a very high barrier business, limited competition, and also a limited customer base (primarily Boeing and Airbus). It supplies high-performance commercial and military engines, which have over the years turned into elements of national interest as the tensions, in particular trade wars, caused a series of import-export restrictions between China and the US (critical earth minerals, semiconductor chips, etc.).

The importance of ATI in the Aerospace & Defense sector has translated into its financials in the last two years. Indeed, the earnings of the company, as reflected by its stock price, were relatively flat from 1996 to 2022, and started to rally only in the aftermath of the Ukraine-Russia war that disrupted many supply chains.

The stock hit its first all-time high in 2026, its first all-time high since 2007, as sector tailwinds and a shift in global supply dynamics significantly improved the company’s fundamentals, turning it into an essential company for the US and Western aerospace. The company has turned into an important player as a supplier of jet engines (39% of ATI revenue as per the latest Q3 2025 earnings report), airframes, and new defense programs requiring high performance materials.

Hard Figures

The strong demands for the components mentioned above have translated into solid financials, with strong revenue and earnings growth reported in Q3 2025 (the one for Q4 is expected in February). The company reported 7% YoY revenue growth (above its 5-year average revenue growth of 5%) and a 42% spike in earnings per share with a $0.85 per share reported. It’s a relatively profitable company despite the high capex (high barrier) segment with a net income margin of 9.7%, again above its 5-year average when the company was primarily in loss with a -5.6% negative net income margin.

Return on equity exploded to 25%, up from its -7% 5-year average ROE, demonstrating a company’s fundamentals can change rapidly when the macro picture aligns with the business. The change in leadership with the new CEO Kimberly Fields (CEO since 1st of July, 2024) is also a shift with her emphasis on profitability and focus on business segments where demand (and growth potential) is higher. The segment growing the fastest (Jet Engine) is interestingly also the largest by revenue share (39%), demonstrating a common-sense strategy of the new CEO.

On another note, the company does not pay dividends (yet), meaning it’s not bound yet by dividend payments, allowing it some leeway and favoring discretionary share repurchases. Management indeed clearly prefers share repurchases and repurchased approximately two million shares for $150 million in Q3 alone, and an additional $120 million remains available on the current authorization. This brings the total share repurchase YTD to 6.4 million shares for $470 million.

The balance sheet of the company is relatively pristine despite years of financial losses and a capex-heavy segment. With a comfortable cash pile of $372 million, total assets of $5.0 billion surpass the $3.2 billion of total liabilities, allowing the company some leeway in its capital deployment in further share buybacks and strategic capex spending in the business segments with the highest growth rates.

A Stretched Valuation

While the fundamentals appear particularly solid, the financials are healthy, and the overall global picture favors US-based suppliers to the Western customers Boeing and Airbus amidst trade restrictions, the valuation is perhaps the only point that keeps me from rating the stock a Strong Buy.

Indeed, ATI is trading at a lofty 39x 2025 earnings and even on a forward basis is trading at 32x 2026 earnings, reflecting high earnings growth priced in but also a valuation more akin to tech growth stocks. Needless to mention, the stock is trading at significantly higher multiples than its 5-year historical average of 21x earnings and 1.5x sales, with the stock now hitting a P/S of 3.8x. From a P/B, the stock is also trading at 9.9x, above its 5.1x 5-year historical average. Of course, fundamentals have changed in a significant way, and the company is now profitable and growing, while previously it was a loss maker with flatline revenue. A good comparable in the absence of US peers producing the same alloys for the same customers is the sector median of Aerospace and Defense. According to Seeking Alpha data, the sector median for the sector is closer to 26x earnings and 2x price to sales. While it’s unlikely ATI will lose its momentum in the near future given the strength of the tailwinds favoring its niche, I would, as an investor, wait for pullbacks along the trajectory to accumulate more shares.

Key Risk Factors to My Buy Case

There are two key risk factors that could invalidate my bullish stance on the stock: one is supply-related, and the other is demand related.

Indeed, while the demand is currently strong (and assuming it remains strong backed by multiyear tailwinds), there is simply only so much ATI can process and execute. In other terms, the production capacity as well as the operational expansion are constrained, especially if ATI wants to maintain quality and avoid any adverse media in relation to the jet engines supplied to Boeing and Airbus, but also other product lines. To a large extent, the way ATI manages to expand its operations will dictate the growth pace of the business.

The other key risk factor is the cut in defense spending. While the conversation has been muted somewhat for a few months, one of the early hopes of the Trump administration was to cut defense spending in half (if Russia and China did so as well). However, this factor is mitigated in my view, as he called for a defense spending increase of 50% just last week.

Bottom Line

ATI is a solid company with the stars clearly aligned to support its growth and further stock price appreciation. The stark valuation is the key reason I am rating the stock a Buy instead of a Strong Buy, and in the absence of identical peers, any adverse news relative to these tailwinds could significantly reprice the stock. I expect some pullback after a +123% rally in the last twelve months, which could offer a great price entry (or accumulation if you are already a shareholder) in a stock that could continue outperforming the broader sector and markets.

data.title: Corning: Executing Well, But The Stock Is Priced Well Ahead Of It
data.description:
I’m initiating coverage on Corning Incorporated (GLW) at a time when the business is doing many things right. Over the past year, the company has really tightened its execution, improved margins, and repositioned its growth profile.

Today, its Optical Communications segment is still the dominant driver of revenue and profit growth. Even more importantly, though, is how its Springboard plan is showing up in the numbers. It certainly looks like it’s been a major success, one that is crucial to how I view the company moving forward.

Having said that, I’m going with a hold on Corning’s stock for now, and the issue is timing. In my view, the stock reflects a huge part of that improvement already. If you’re holding the stock today, it means that you have really high expectations of what Corning can and will do over the next few years, and the margin for error is very narrow.

At nearly $90/share, I believe that the valuation is full, and the combination of everything I have listed above makes this a good moment to step back, lay out what Corning actually is today, and decide whether the current setup justifies fresh capital.

Company Overview

According to most sources, along with the company’s own documents, Corning is the world’s biggest materials science and advanced manufacturing company by revenue, making fiber, cable, and multi-fiber connectors, among other things. It made ~$12 billion in net sales in the first three quarters of FY25.

Side note: Before covering it, I only really knew the company as the manufacturer of the Gorilla Glass that comes with all my Samsung Galaxy devices (I’m a big Galaxy aficionado, no Apple for me).

Corning runs five distinct operating segments, although it reports operations for its emerging businesses (solar & semiconductor technologies, along with pharmaceutical technologies & emerging innovations) under the Hemlock and Emerging Growth Businesses umbrella. More importantly, though, the company’s business revolves around selling critical components into industries where reliability, scale, and long qualification cycles matter more than speed.

In my opinion, the business today looks meaningfully different from how many investors may think about it, especially compared to a few years ago.

For one, Optical Communications is now the largest segment. This includes the fiber, cable, and connectivity solutions that it sells into telecom networks, broadband builds, enterprise infrastructure, and increasingly, AI-driven data centers.

Display Technologies is the next biggest contributor, but it doesn’t define the company anymore. This segment supplies glass to TV, monitor, and laptop panel makers. From the look of things, demand in this segment is mature and cyclical (there are only so many devices and screens we can look at daily), and I think that management will focus more on pricing discipline and margin protection rather than volume growth moving forward.

Then, there is the Specialty Materials segment. This is where Corning makes the Gorilla Glass and the other advanced glass products that are used primarily in premium consumer electronics.

Automotive (formerly Environmental Technologies) supplies the ceramic substrates and filters used in emissions control systems for automotive and heavy-duty vehicles.

Finally, we have the Life Sciences segment, the smallest one. This segment sells labware and cell culture products into biopharma, academic, and industrial labs.

Corning’s Moat And Growth Drivers

When I zoom out, Corning’s product stack is incredibly broad, but the monetization logic is consistent. Since it sells components that sit deep inside customer systems, they tend to stick around for years because the qualification cycles are long and switching costs are real. Think about the company’s $2.5 billion investment from Apple to make 100% of all the glass used for the latter’s iPhones and Apple Watches globally over the next few years in its Kentucky plant. Obviously, the deal has a lot more to it than just the optics of two long-time partners renewing a relationship. There’s the geopolitical benefit of having production closer to home for Apple, given its exposure in Asia, but it demonstrates something else for Corning: staying power. It’s just one example, but deals like these are what give the business its durability. At the same time, it also means that its growth is tied to customer capex cycles and end-market demand, not to quick wins.

In my opinion, Optical Communications is the clearest example. This segment sells fiber and connectivity solutions into two demand pools that don’t move the same way: telecom carriers and broadband builders on one side, and enterprise and hyperscale customers on the other. We’re seeing a difference in the current cycle because enterprise demand is being pulled forward by the higher fiber density requirements and new GenAI-related connectivity needs. If you look at the company’s Q3 FY25 earnings report, you’ll see that its Optical enterprise sales grew 58% year over year in Q3 because of strong adoption of its GenAI products.

It’s a different story for Display Technologies too, now. Corning sells primarily to a small number of large panel makers, and those volumes are still cyclical. I’d say that the segment behaves more like a cash engine and margin stabilizer than a growth engine, and the Q3 numbers reflect that dynamic.

Specialty Materials is where Corning’s premium glass relationships show up. Customers here care about durability and optical performance, and when you win a design, it is typically going to stay for several years. I’m thinking about Samsung while writing this, but it’s also easy to see that Apple’s ecosystem is central to how many investors think about this segment. I mentioned the $2.5 billion commitment earlier, and the word from management seems to be that the deal is an incremental growth opportunity through 2026 and beyond.

Looking at the last three major segments, I’d describe Automotive as a steady, regulation-influenced business. Once it wins a platform, the content is relatively stable over a vehicle program, so the story here is usually more about steady execution than upside surprise. The same goes for Life Sciences, although the demand there comes from consumables and lab demand. It’s not a growth engine, but it does its job well for Corning’s overall profile.

Hemlock & Emerging Growth Businesses is the interesting segment, in my opinion. Here, the sales have mostly come from solar technology, and management is very confident that it can turn its solar business into a $2.5 billion sales machine by 2028. It made $200 million in sales in Q1 FY25, and the figure was around that mark in Q2. In Q3, though, it was well over $300 million, although some of that came from semiconductor sales. Still, we can see the size of the opportunity there.

On the Q3 earnings call, management said that it plans to produce well over 1 million wafers daily in Q4 FY25 after it finished building “the largest solar ingot and wafer facility in the United States, co-located with our polysilicon manufacturing facility in Hemlock, Michigan.” That’s not a small announcement, and when you factor in the fact that it has customers committed to using over 80% of its capacity over the next 5 years, it’s hard not to believe that it can hit the $2.5 billion target in 2028.

Springboard Is The Bridge

Springboard is Corning’s execution system for turning these demand trends into real financial outcomes. The plan centers on two outcomes by the end of 2026:

  1. Add over $4 billion in annualized sales, and

  2. Reach a 20% operating margin.

The company started this plan in Q4 FY23, and where it gets interesting is that Springboard is already showing results much earlier than planned. In the Q3 2025 earnings release, management said the company has added $4 billion to its annualized sales run rate and expects to reach the 20% operating margin target in Q4 2025, a full year ahead of schedule.

The new plan is to hit $6 billion by Q4 2026, and at this rate, it is definitely feasible. But a lot has to keep going right. So when I talk about Corning’s growth and operating profiles changing, I’m not talking about a new identity. I’m talking about a portfolio where growth and tighter execution are now doing the heavy lifting, and the company’s own operating system (Springboard) is translating that into margin and cash flow improvements. Now, let’s look at the numbers.

The Numbers Are Great

In my opinion, the financials back up most of what management is saying, but they also define the boundaries of the opportunity, for now. The cleanest way to see what has changed is to look at core sales progression through FY25, which strips out the noise so we can see the business that Corning actually manages.

In the table above, we can see that sales increase sequentially across all three quarters, and it is a clear progression from FY2024. So, it’s not a rebound. More importantly, the growth is coming from product mix and pricing, although Optical Communications is doing most of the work.
